openapi.label: OpenAPI
openapi.description: REST API descriptions — paths, operations, schemas, and security.

rules.name: Array Items
rules.description: Array Items.

rules.name: Channel Parameter Require Examples
rules.description: Parameter must have examples.

rules.name: Component Example Keys Snake Case
rules.description: example key must be snake cased (e.g. snake_case).

rules.name: Component Message Require Examples
rules.description: Message must have examples.

rules.name: Component Require Security Schemes
rules.description: The API contract MUST include a 'securitySchemes' subsection under the 'components' section.

rules.name: Contact Properties
rules.description: Contact object must have "name", "url" and "email".

rules.name: Document Cache Control Parameter
rules.description: Cache usage SHOULD be extensively detailed in the `description` property to avoid data leaks or the usage of stale data. This rule should ensure in some way that the api provider documented extensively the cache usage to avoid data leaks or usage of stale data. For now this ruleset tests: * the presence of following keywords in the `description`: `max-age`, `private`, `no-store`, `no-cache`. * that one and only one between Expires and Cache-Control is used. `Cache-Control` and `Expires` should not be used in conjuction, because `Cache-Control` overrides `Expires` when `max-age` is set. Instead if neither `Cache-Control` or `Expires` are set, clients MAY use euristic cache like described in RFC7234.

rules.name: Document Cache Response Header
rules.description: Cache usage SHOULD be extensively detailed in the `description` property to avoid data leaks or the usage of stale data. This rule should ensure in some way that the api provider documented extensively the cache usage to avoid data leaks or usage of stale data. For now this ruleset tests: * the presence of following keywords in the `description`: `max-age`, `private`, `no-store`, `no-cache`. * that one and only one between Expires and Cache-Control is used. `Cache-Control` and `Expires` should not be used in conjuction, because `Cache-Control` overrides `Expires` when `max-age` is set. Instead if neither `Cache-Control` or `Expires` are set, clients MAY use euristic cache like described in RFC7234.

rules.name: Document OWASP API9 Server
rules.description: The API should declare its servers so all hosts and environments are inventoried — undocumented or stray non-production hosts are a common inventory-management risk (OWASP API9).

rules.name: Document Require Component Section
rules.description: The API contract MUST include a 'components' section.

rules.name: Document Require External Docs
rules.description: Defining external documentation provides a link to detailed API guides, tutorials, and reference material beyond the OpenAPI spec. Analysis shows 57.1% of APIs include external docs.

rules.name: Document Require Jsonschemadialect
rules.description: openapi document should declare a `jsonSchemaDialect` property.

rules.name: Document Require Openapi
rules.description: openapi document should declare a `openapi` property.

rules.name: Document Require Path
rules.description: openapi document should declare a `paths` property.

rules.name: Document Require Realistic Examples
rules.description: API design SHOULD include real-like examples for request and response definitions.

rules.name: Document Require Server Defined
rules.description: Every OpenAPI should define at least one server URL. Analysis shows 97.2% of APIs define servers, providing consumers with the base URL needed to make requests.

rules.name: Document Require Webhooks
rules.description: openapi document should declare a `webhooks` property.

rules.name: Document Spec Must Not Be Empty
rules.description: Spec should not be empty.

rules.name: Duplicated Entry In Enum
rules.description: Enum values must not have duplicate entry.

rules.name: Header Names Hyphenated Pascal Case
rules.description: All `HTTP` headers MUST use `Hyphenated-Pascal-Case` notation.

rules.name: Header No X Prefix
rules.description: 'HTTP' headers SHOULD NOT start with 'X-' RFC6648.

rules.name: Header Require Examples
rules.description: Headers must include examples.

rules.name: Info Contact
rules.description: Info object must have "contact" object.

rules.name: Info Contact Require Email
rules.description: Having a contact email address associated with the technical contract ensures that anyone who comes across the API has someone to email and get more information.

rules.name: Info Contact Require Name
rules.description: Having a contact name associated with the technical contract ensures that anyone who comes across the API knows who to contact.

rules.name: Info Contact Require URL
rules.description: Having a contact url associated with the technical contract ensures that anyone who comes across the API knows where to go to contact someone.

rules.name: Info Description
rules.description: Info "description" must be present and non-empty string.

rules.name: Info Description Length Limit
rules.description: Having a restriction on the length of the API description expressed as the OpenAPI info description helps provide constraints for consumers when adding a description, and keeps portals, landing pages, documentation, and discovery results more consistent.

rules.name: Info Description No Eval Tag
rules.description: Eval functions MUST not be included in the description of an API, keeping descriptions to just the text that is needed, and relying on the rest of the OpenAPI to describe what is possible.

rules.name: Info Description No Script Tag
rules.description: Script tags MUST not be included in the description of an API, keeping descriptions to just the text that is needed, and relying on the rest of the OpenAPI to describe what is possible.

rules.name: Info License
rules.description: Info object must have "license" object.

rules.name: Info License Require Name
rules.description: The license object should include a name property identifying the license type, such as Apache 2.0, MIT, or a proprietary license.

rules.name: Info License Require URL
rules.description: The license object should include a URL linking to the full license text so API consumers can review the terms.

rules.name: Info Microcks Extension Valid
rules.description: x-microcks extension must be valid.

rules.name: Info OWASP API9 Document Version
rules.description: The API should declare a version in info.version so every published version is inventoried and retired versions can be tracked (OWASP API9 — improper inventory management).

rules.name: Info Require API Identifier
rules.description: The `#/info/x-api-id` field can be used to associate an identifier to an API. This is useful to track an API even when its `#/info/title` changes.

rules.name: Info Require Contact 2
rules.description: Having a contact object associated with the technical contract ensures that anyone who comes across the API has someone to contact and get more information.

rules.name: Info Require Description
rules.description: Info section is missing a description.

rules.name: Info Require License
rules.description: Having a license defined in the info object clarifies the terms under which the API can be used. Analysis shows only 44.4% of APIs define a license, but it is essential for API governance and compliance.

rules.name: Info Require Openapi Version 3x
rules.description: APIs should use OpenAPI 3.x specification. Analysis of 773 API specs shows 97% use OpenAPI 3.x, with 86% on 3.1.0, making it the dominant standard for modern API definitions.

rules.name: Info Require Summary
rules.description: The `#/info/x-summary` can be used to specify a brief, one-liner description of your API: this is very useful for catalog purposes (eg. this can be shown as your API subtitle in catalogs and developer portals). In OAS3.1 you can use the standard `#/info/summary` field.

rules.name: Info Require Terms Of Service
rules.description: API MUST reference the URL of the Terms of Service in `#/info/termsOfService`.

rules.name: Info Require Title
rules.description: Having a intuitive and helpful title for your API using the OpenAPI info title is the first impression you will make on the consumers of your API.

rules.name: Info Require Version
rules.description: Publishing a version for your OpenAPI technical contract helps you communicate change with consumers using Semantic or date-based versioning published to the info version property.

rules.name: Info Title Capitalized
rules.description: Having a consistent casing for the title for your API helps provide constraints for teams naming the API, but also keep consistent with other APIs from across teams.

rules.name: Info Title Max Length
rules.description: Having a limitation on the length of the title for your API helps provide constraints for teams naming it, but also keep consistent with other APIs from across teams.

rules.name: Info Version Must Be Semver
rules.description: The API version field should follow [semantic versioning](https://semver.org/#semantic-versioning-specification-semver).

rules.name: Info Version Must Use Semver
rules.description: Version should use semantic versioning. {{value}} is not a valid version.

rules.name: Info Version Semantic And Stable
rules.description: The API contract MUST have a stable version and MUST follow semantic versioning (e.g., '1.0.0'). Words like 'SNAPSHOT' or 'RELEASE' are not allowed.

rules.name: License URL
rules.description: License object must include "url".

rules.name: Limit Nested Sub Resources
rules.description: The hierarchy of nested resources SHOULD NOT contain more than 8 resource names in the path.

rules.name: Limit Path Length
rules.description: APIs SHOULD NOT expand their total URL length beyond a few hundred characters.

rules.name: Limit Path Parameter
rules.description: The URL path should not contain more than 3 dynamic path parameters.

rules.name: Message Examples Form Complete Mocks
rules.description: Message example must match params examples to form full mocks.

rules.name: Message Require Examples
rules.description: Message must have examples.

rules.name: No $ref Siblings
rules.description: Property must not be placed among $ref.

rules.name: No Eval In Markdown
rules.description: Markdown descriptions must not have "eval(".

rules.name: No Script Tags In Markdown
rules.description: Markdown descriptions must not have "<script>" tags.

rules.name: OAS3 API Servers
rules.description: OpenAPI "servers" must be present and non-empty array.

rules.name: OAS3 Callbacks In Callbacks
rules.description: OAS3 Callbacks In Callbacks.

rules.name: OAS3 Examples Value Or ExternalValue
rules.description: Examples must have either "value" or "externalValue" field.

rules.name: OAS3 Operation Security Defined
rules.description: Operation "security" values must match a scheme defined in the "components.securitySchemes" object.

rules.name: OAS3 Parameter Description
rules.description: Parameter objects must have "description".

rules.name: OAS3 Schema
rules.description: Validate structure of OpenAPI v3 specification.

rules.name: OAS3 Server Not Example.com
rules.description: Server URL must not point at example.com.

rules.name: OAS3 Server Trailing Slash
rules.description: Server URL must not have trailing slash.

rules.name: OAS3 Server Variables
rules.description: Server variables must be defined and valid and there must be no unused variables.

rules.name: OAS3 Unused Component
rules.description: OAS3 Unused Component.

rules.name: OAS3 Valid Media Example
rules.description: Examples must be valid against their defined schema.

rules.name: OAS3 Valid Schema Example
rules.description: Examples must be valid against their defined schema.

rules.name: OAS3.1 Callbacks In Webhook
rules.description: OAS3.1 Callbacks In Webhook.

rules.name: OAS3.1 Servers In Webhook
rules.description: OAS3.1 Servers In Webhook.

rules.name: Openapi Tags
rules.description: OpenAPI object must have non-empty "tags" array.

rules.name: Openapi Tags Alphabetical
rules.description: OpenAPI object must have alphabetical "tags".

rules.name: Openapi Tags Uniqueness
rules.description: Each tag must have a unique name.

rules.name: Operation Collection Support Filtering
rules.description: Collection GET operations should support filtering via a filter query parameter (e.g. filter or filter[field]).

rules.name: Operation Collection Support Sorting
rules.description: Collection GET operations should support sorting via a sort or order query parameter.

rules.name: Operation Collection Support Sparse Fieldsets
rules.description: GET operations should support sparse fieldsets via a fields query parameter so clients can request only the fields they need.

rules.name: Operation Define 429 Response
rules.description: APIs should define a 429 Too Many Requests response to document rate limiting behavior. Analysis shows 429 appears in 671 operations, confirming rate limiting is a common API pattern.

rules.name: Operation Define 500 Response
rules.description: Every endpoint SHOULD have a 500 response.

rules.name: Operation Deprecated Require Description
rules.description: Deprecated operations should have a description explaining the deprecation and the migration path / successor.

rules.name: Operation Description
rules.description: Operation "description" must be present and non-empty string.

rules.name: Operation Description Max Length
rules.description: Having a length limitation for each description of each API operation helps apply constraints to how you describe your APIs, while helping drive consistency across APIs when it comes to search, documentation, and other ways an API is made available.

rules.name: Operation Examples Form Complete Mocks
rules.description: Response example must match required params examples to form full mocks.

rules.name: Operation Get Require Security
rules.description: Your API should be protected by a `security` rule either at global or operation level. Operations should be protected specially when they are tied to non-idempotent HTTP methods like `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH` and `DELETE`. This is done with one or more non-empty `security` rules. Security rules are defined in the `securityScheme` section. An example of a security rule applied at global level. ``` security: - BasicAuth: [] paths: /books: {} /users: {} securitySchemes: BasicAuth: scheme: http type: basic ``` An example of a security rule applied at operation level, which eventually overrides the global one ``` paths: /books: post: security: - AccessToken: [] securitySchemes: BasicAuth: scheme: http type: basic AccessToken: scheme: http type: bearer bearerFormat: JWT ```.

rules.name: Operation Get Support If Modified Since
rules.description: GET operations should accept an If-Modified-Since request header to enable date-based conditional requests.

rules.name: Operation Get Support If None Match
rules.description: GET operations should accept an If-None-Match request header to enable ETag-based conditional requests (304 responses).

rules.name: Operation ID Camel Case
rules.description: Operation identifiers provide a unique way to identify each individual API, and requiring them to have consistent casing reduces friction when generating SDKs and automating around APIs.

rules.name: Operation ID URL Safe
rules.description: OperationId must use URL friendly characters.

rules.name: Operation Microcks Extension Valid
rules.description: x-microcks-operation extension must be valid.

rules.name: Operation Mutation Support Idempotency Key
rules.description: POST and PATCH operations should accept an Idempotency-Key request header so clients can safely retry without creating duplicates.

rules.name: Operation OperationId
rules.description: Operation must have "operationId".

rules.name: Operation OperationId Unique
rules.description: Every operation must have unique "operationId".

rules.name: Operation OperationId Valid In URL
rules.description: Operation OperationId Valid In URL.

rules.name: Operation Parameters
rules.description: Operation parameters are unique and non-repeating.

rules.name: Operation Patch Must Have Request Body
rules.description: PATCH operations must have a request body.

rules.name: Operation Post Not For Retrieval
rules.description: POST requests SHOULD NOT be used for retrieving information. Use GET instead.

rules.name: Operation Post Require Request Body
rules.description: POST HTTP methods can have a request body, providing a structured payload for configuring each API request.

rules.name: Operation Put Require Request Body
rules.description: PUT requests MUST have a request body.

rules.name: Operation Ref Resource File
rules.description: Endpoint must a $ref to a file in resources/.

rules.name: Operation Require Description
rules.description: Having a paragraph or two description of each API operation helps API consumers understand what is possible with each API request.

rules.name: Operation Require ID
rules.description: Every operation should have an operationId for SDK generation and documentation linking. Analysis shows operationId styles across 8878 operations with camelCase dominant at 92%.

rules.name: Operation Require Identifier
rules.description: Operation identifiers provide a unique way to identify each individual API, which then used for SDK generation and other automation.

rules.name: Operation Require One Tag
rules.description: Having tags applied to each API operations helps organize and group APIs in portals, documentation, search, and other ways in which APIs are made available.

rules.name: Operation Require Response
rules.description: Check if every request has their respective responses.

rules.name: Operation Require Security
rules.description: Check operation security is defined.

rules.name: Operation Require Security 2
rules.description: Each API operation should have a security definition referencing the central security scheme express for an OpenAPI.

rules.name: Operation Require Summary
rules.description: Having short and intuitive summary for each API operation helps API consumers understand what is possible with each API request.

rules.name: Operation Require Tag
rules.description: Having tags applied to each API operations helps organize and group APIs in portals, documentation, search, and other ways in which APIs are made available.

rules.name: Operation Security Use Defined Scheme
rules.description: Check operation security uses a defined security scheme.

rules.name: Operation Singular Tag
rules.description: Operation must not have more than a single tag.

rules.name: Operation Success Response
rules.description: Operation must have at least one "2xx" or "3xx" response.

rules.name: Operation Summary Description Differ
rules.description: Operation summaries and description should not match.

rules.name: Operation Summary Max Length
rules.description: Apply length constraints to the operation summary helps keep them consistent for publishing in documentation.

rules.name: Operation Summary No Period
rules.description: Operation summaries should not have a period, keeping the primary summary for each API as consistent as possible for publishing in documentation.

rules.name: Operation Tag Defined
rules.description: Operation tags must be defined in global tags.

rules.name: Operation Tag Title Case
rules.description: Having the first letter of each word applied as a tag to API operations helps keep a consistent layout when published via search, documentation, and other ways APIs are made available.

rules.name: Operation Tags
rules.description: Operation must have non-empty "tags" array.

rules.name: Operation Use Ref
rules.description: Endpoint must be a $ref.

rules.name: Operation Well Understood Status Codes
rules.description: MUST use standard HTTP status codes [150].

rules.name: Operation Write Support If Match
rules.description: Write operations should accept an If-Match request header to enable optimistic concurrency / conditional writes.

rules.name: Operation Write Support If Unmodified Since
rules.description: Write operations should accept an If-Unmodified-Since request header to enable date-based optimistic concurrency / conditional writes.

rules.name: Parameter Array Require Items
rules.description: Parameters that are of an array type should always have the items defined, being explicit about what is continued as part of the array.

rules.name: Parameter Array Typed Items
rules.description: Array parameters must have an items attribute with a type.

rules.name: Parameter Avoid Integer ID
rules.description: Avoid exposing IDs as an integer, UUIDs or other interoperable strings are preferred.

rules.name: Parameter Collection Limit Param Default
rules.description: The limit / page-size query parameter should declare a default so the page size is predictable when the caller omits it.

rules.name: Parameter Collection Limit Param Maximum
rules.description: The limit / page-size query parameter should declare a maximum so a caller cannot request an unbounded page (OWASP API4 — unrestricted resource consumption).

rules.name: Parameter Deprecated Require Description
rules.description: Deprecated parameters should have a description explaining the deprecation and what to use instead.

rules.name: Parameter Description Max Length
rules.description: Limiting the length of parameters description forces us to be more concise in how we describe each parameter, while keeping our documentation and other ways descriptions show up in discovery and portals more consistent.

rules.name: Parameter Enum Upper Snake Case
rules.description: Keeping parameters enumerator casing consistent across APIs helps reduce confusion by consumers, and can keep aligned with services and applications putting an API to work.

rules.name: Parameter Filter Param Only On Get
rules.description: Only GET-based endpoints SHOULD have have the query parameter 'filter'.

rules.name: Parameter Integer Require Maximum
rules.description: Parameters that are of the integer schema type must have their maximum value set, defining the shape of parameter data passed in with a request.

rules.name: Parameter Integer Require Minimum
rules.description: Parameters that are of the integer schema type must have their minimum value set, defining the shape of parameter data passed in with a request.

rules.name: Parameter Keys Snake Case
rules.description: parameter key must be snake cased (e.g. snake_case).

rules.name: Parameter Name Max Length
rules.description: Providing short and concise names for your parameters helps make it easier for API consumers to understand how they are able to configure their API requests.

rules.name: Parameter Names Camel Case
rules.description: Providing parameters with consistent naming helps make it easier for API consumers to understand how they are able to configure their API requests.

rules.name: Parameter No API Keys In Query
rules.description: Query parameters MUST not contain sensitive information, like API tokens or keys.

rules.name: Parameter No Forbidden HTTP Header
rules.description: OAS do not allow using the following HTTP headers in a specification file: Authorization, Content-Type and Accept. You MUST use the associate functionalities provided by OAS, instead.

rules.name: Parameter No Hybrid And Root Filtering
rules.description: Hybrid filtering MAY be offered on multiple attributes, but MUST never exist if a root "filter" query parameter is present.

rules.name: Parameter No ID Query
rules.description: Resource identifier filtering is not allowed as a query parameter. Use the resource identifier in the URL path.

rules.name: Parameter No X Prefixed Request Header
rules.description: Do not use headers with X-.

rules.name: Parameter Optional Include Examples
rules.description: Optional param should have examples.

rules.name: Parameter OWASP API1 No Numeric IDs
rules.description: Path parameters should not be sequential integers (BOLA / object enumeration, OWASP API1) — use a non-guessable identifier such as a UUID.

rules.name: Parameter Pagination Params In Query
rules.description: Pagination parameters like page, limit, offset, and cursor should be passed as query parameters, not in headers or path segments. Analysis confirms 100% of pagination parameters observed are query parameters.

rules.name: Parameter Query Allowed Characters
rules.description: Query parameter keys MUST include only alpha-numeric characters and periods: [Aa0-Zz9]'.

rules.name: Parameter Query Camel Case
rules.description: MUST query parameter names must be ASCII camelCase [130a].

rules.name: Parameter Query Must Be Optional
rules.description: Query parameters MUST be optional.

rules.name: Parameter Require Description
rules.description: Having a parameters description provides more depth to what a parameter does and will be displayed via documentation, and other tooling used across the API lifecycle.

rules.name: Parameter Require Examples
rules.description: Parameters must include examples.

rules.name: Parameter Require In
rules.description: Providing an in property for parameters gets explicit about whether a parameter is in the path, query, or a header, making it clear to consumers where they can configure their request.

rules.name: Parameter Require Name
rules.description: Providing a simple, intuitive, and consistent names for your parameters helps make it easier for API consumers to understand how they are able to configure their API requests.

rules.name: Parameter Require Required Flag
rules.description: Providrequiredg an required property for parameters gets explicit about whether a parameter is required the path, query, or a header, making it clear to consumers where they can configure their request.

rules.name: Parameter Require Schema
rules.description: Parameters must always possess a schema to help define the format and shape of the parameter, setting expections with consumers about what should be passed in.

rules.name: Parameter Required Require Examples
rules.description: Required param must have examples.

rules.name: Parameter Schema Require Type
rules.description: Parameters must always have their schema type defined, being precise about what type of data can be inputted and used to configure an API request.

rules.name: Parameter Schema Use Ref
rules.description: Parameters must always use a schema reference that utilizes reusable schema that are defined as part of a centralized schema components library.

rules.name: Parameter Sort Param Only On Get
rules.description: Non-GET endpoints MUST NOT have sorting query parameters. Parameter names such as sort, sorting, orderBy, etc.

rules.name: Parameter String Require Max Length
rules.description: Parameters that are of the string schema type must have their maximum value set, defining the shape of parameter data passed in with a request.

rules.name: Parameter String Require Min Length
rules.description: Parameters that are of the string schema type must have their minimum value set, defining the shape of parameter data passed in with a request.

rules.name: Parameter String Require Pattern
rules.description: Parameters that are of the string schema type must have a pattern set, using a regex to define the shape of parameter data passed in with a request.

rules.name: Path CORS Support Options Preflight
rules.description: Resources should expose an OPTIONS operation so browsers can complete a CORS preflight. (Many teams handle preflight at the gateway and treat CORS as operational — enable this only if you document CORS in the spec.).

rules.name: Path Declarations Must Exist
rules.description: Path Declarations Must Exist.

rules.name: Path Delete No Response Body
rules.description: Check if the delete response does not have any body.

rules.name: Path Get Response Support JSON
rules.description: All APIs returning structured data SHOULD support JSON as the default format.

rules.name: Path Include Version
rules.description: Path must include the version.

rules.name: Path Keys No Trailing Slash
rules.description: Path Keys No Trailing Slash.

rules.name: Path Max Nesting Depth
rules.description: API paths should not exceed 4 levels of nesting depth. Analysis of 6627 paths across 773 specs shows an average depth of 3.04 segments, with deeper paths indicating overly complex resource hierarchies.

rules.name: Path No API Prefix In
rules.description: A resource SHOULD NOT contain 'api' as a prefix in or a part of the path.

rules.name: Path No Empty Segment
rules.description: A resource MUST use normalized paths without empty path segments.

rules.name: Path No Environment Names In
rules.description: API paths MUST NOT indicate environment names.

rules.name: Path No File Extension In
rules.description: A resource SHOULD NOT make use of an extension at any point in the path.

rules.name: Path No File Extensions
rules.description: API paths should not include file extensions like .json or .xml. Content negotiation should be handled through Accept headers rather than path suffixes.

rules.name: Path No Format Extensions In
rules.description: Request and Response media type formats MUST NOT be implied using extensions on resources (i.e. .json or .xml). Instead, use the standard resource path with the appropriate Content-Type header.

rules.name: Path No HTTP Method Names
rules.description: When you design a REST API, you don't usually need to mention terms like `get`, `delete` and so on in your `paths`, because this information is conveyed by the HTTP method. Instead of using ``` POST /books/1234/delete HTTP/1.1 Host: api.example ``` You can simply call ``` DELETE /books/1234 HTTP/1.1 Host: api.example ``` Similarly you don't need verbs like `list` or `create` because the HTTP Semantics RFC7231 supports this kind of actions natively with proper methods and status code. Instead of ``` POST /create/user HTTP/1.1 Host: api.example Content-Type: application/json {"given_name": "Mario"} ``` You can use ``` POST /create/user HTTP/1.1 Host: api.example Content-Type: application/json {"given_name": "Mario"} ``` returning a proper response ``` HTTP/1.1 201 Created Location: /users/1234 ``` This simplifies securing your API as you know beforehand the kind of action which is going to be performed.

rules.name: Path No HTTP Methods In
rules.description: A resource SHOULD NOT contain HTTP methods.

rules.name: Path No HTTP Verbs
rules.description: HTTP verbs should be not be in URL paths.

rules.name: Path No Invalid Symbols
rules.description: Check if the given endpoint has queryparameter.

rules.name: Path No Query String
rules.description: Path must not include query string.

rules.name: Path No Query Strings In
rules.description: Paths SHOULD NOT have query parameters in them. They should be defined separately in the OpenAPI.

rules.name: Path No Technology Leak In
rules.description: A resource MUST NOT leak or expose format or technology-specific information at any point in the path.

rules.name: Path No Trailing Slash
rules.description: Path must not end with a slash.

rules.name: Path No Trailing Slash In
rules.description: A resource MUST be addressable without a trailing slash on the path.

rules.name: Path No Verbs
rules.description: API paths MUST be resource-focused and MUST NOT include verbs like 'get', 'update', 'create', or 'delete'.

rules.name: Path No Version Segment
rules.description: API paths should not contain version numbers like v1 or v2. API versioning should be handled through headers, query parameters, or server URLs rather than embedded in resource paths.

rules.name: Path No X Prefixed Response Header
rules.description: Do not use headers with X-.

rules.name: Path Not Include Query
rules.description: Path must not include query string.

rules.name: Path Parameter Camel Case
rules.description: Path parameters MUST follow camelCase.

rules.name: Path Parameter Constant Case
rules.description: Path parameters MUST follow CONSTANT_CASE (screaming snake_case).

rules.name: Path Parameter Kebab Case
rules.description: Path parameters MUST follow kebab-case.

rules.name: Path Parameter Not Empty
rules.description: Path parameter declarations must not be empty ex. `/api/{}` is invalid.

rules.name: Path Parameter Pascal Case
rules.description: Path parameters MUST follow PascalCase.

rules.name: Path Parameter Snake Case
rules.description: Path parameters MUST follow snake_case.

rules.name: Path Params
rules.description: Path parameters must be defined and valid.

rules.name: Path Params Declared In Block
rules.description: Check for the path parameter in the parameter block.

rules.name: Path Require Query Parameter
rules.description: Check if the given resource path has queryparameter.

rules.name: Path Require Tracing Header
rules.description: MUST use b3 or w3c tracing [233a].

rules.name: Path Require Valid HTTP Verb
rules.description: All APIs MUST have a valid http verb.

rules.name: Path Resources Plural
rules.description: Resource names in paths should use plural nouns to maintain consistency and follow REST conventions for collection-oriented resource design.

rules.name: Path Segment Kebab Case
rules.description: All YAML/JSON paths MUST follow kebab-case.

rules.name: Path Success Response Require Description
rules.description: Check if the response description is appropriate for all requests.

rules.name: Path URI Versioning Single Version
rules.description: CAN use correct URI versioning [115a].

rules.name: Path Use Standard HTTP Methods
rules.description: Operations MUST use only the common HTTP methods as outlined in the standards guide, and must be in lower-case.

rules.name: Path Write Response Support JSON
rules.description: All APIs returning structured data SHOULD support JSON as the default format.

rules.name: Request Body Must Use JSON Content Type
rules.description: Request bodies should use application/json as the primary content type for consistency across API operations and client implementations.

rules.name: Request Content Require Examples
rules.description: Request with content must have examples.

rules.name: Request Delete Must Not Have Body
rules.description: DELETE operations must not accept a request body.

rules.name: Request Delete No Body
rules.description: DELETE requests MUST NOT have a request body.

rules.name: Request Get Must Not Have Body
rules.description: A `GET` request MUST NOT accept a request body.

rules.name: Request Get No Body
rules.description: GET requests MUST NOT have a request body.

rules.name: Request Head Must Not Have Body
rules.description: A `HEAD` request MUST NOT accept a request body.

rules.name: Request Header Names Pascal Case
rules.description: Headers should be pascal-case. See Italian recommendation RAC_REST_NAME_003.

rules.name: Request Options Must Not Have Body
rules.description: An `OPTIONS` request MUST NOT accept a request body.

rules.name: Request Patch Media Type Not Plain JSON
rules.description: The PATCH specification explicits that the request body contains a "patch document" describing the changes to be applied to the target resource. To avoid confusion, [this errata](https://www.rfc-editor.org/errata/eid3169) explains that `application/json` is not an appropriate media-type for `PATCH`. A correct example of PATCH using eg. `application/json-patch+json` media-type defined in RFC6902. ``` paths: /books/{book_id}: patch: requestBody: content: application/json-patch+json: schema: type: object example: [{ "op": "add", "path": "/baz", "value": "qux" }] ```.

rules.name: Request Post Collection Require Paging Object
rules.description: POST collection endpoints MUST have a request body schema that includes paging parameters.

rules.name: Request Property Names Camel Case
rules.description: MUST property names must be ASCII camelCase [118a].

rules.name: Request Support Accept Encoding
rules.description: Operations should accept an Accept-Encoding request header so clients can negotiate a compressed (e.g. gzip) response.

rules.name: Request Support Accept Language
rules.description: Operations should accept an Accept-Language request header so clients can negotiate a localized response (i18n).

rules.name: Request Support Baggage
rules.description: Operations should accept a baggage request header (W3C Baggage / OpenTelemetry) so application-defined context propagates across services.

rules.name: Request Support Correlation ID
rules.description: Operations should accept a correlation / request ID request header (e.g. Correlation-Id or X-Request-Id) so a caller-supplied id can be traced end to end.

rules.name: Request Support Form Urlencoded Media Type
rules.description: Every request MUST support `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` media type.

rules.name: Request Support JSON Media Type
rules.description: Every request MUST support `application/json` media type.

rules.name: Request Support JSON Media Type 2
rules.description: Every request MUST support `application/json` media type.

rules.name: Request Support Multipart Media Type
rules.description: Every request MUST support `multipart/form-data` media type.

rules.name: Request Support Text Plain Media Type
rules.description: Every request MUST support `text/plain` media type.

rules.name: Request Support Tracestate
rules.description: Operations should accept a tracestate request header (W3C Trace Context) so vendor-specific trace state propagates alongside traceparent.

rules.name: Request Support XML Media Type
rules.description: Every request MUST support `application/xml` media type.

rules.name: Response 202 Location Header
rules.description: 202 Accepted responses should document a Location header pointing at the status or result resource clients can poll for the long-running operation.

rules.name: Response 202 Retry After
rules.description: 202 Accepted responses should document a Retry-After header suggesting how long clients should wait before polling the status resource.

rules.name: Response 206 Require Content Range
rules.description: Check if the get response code 206 has content-type and content-range.

rules.name: Response 400 Require Body
rules.description: All 400 responses must include a response body.

rules.name: Response Body Must Be Object
rules.description: Response bodies must be an object type.

rules.name: Response Cache Header No Conflict
rules.description: Cache usage SHOULD be extensively detailed in the `description` property to avoid data leaks or the usage of stale data. This rule should ensure in some way that the api provider documented extensively the cache usage to avoid data leaks or usage of stale data. For now this ruleset tests: * the presence of following keywords in the `description`: `max-age`, `private`, `no-store`, `no-cache`. * that one and only one between Expires and Cache-Control is used. `Cache-Control` and `Expires` should not be used in conjuction, because `Cache-Control` overrides `Expires` when `max-age` is set. Instead if neither `Cache-Control` or `Expires` are set, clients MAY use euristic cache like described in RFC7234.

rules.name: Response Code Must Be Valid
rules.description: All APIs should return a valid http response code.

rules.name: Response Collection Require Results Array
rules.description: Response bodies must have a root element called results and is an array of objects.

rules.name: Response Collection Support Paging
rules.description: Response bodies from collection endpoints SHOULD offer paging capability.

rules.name: Response Content Require Examples
rules.description: Response with content must have examples.

rules.name: Response CORS Allow Origin No Wildcard
rules.description: If an Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header is documented, it should not hard-code a wildcard (*) — reflect specific allowed origins instead, especially for authenticated APIs (OWASP API8 security misconfiguration). Note: the project treats CORS as operational (see no-operational-headers-in-spec); this is a defensive check for specs that do document it.

rules.name: Response Custom Header Naming Format
rules.description: Custom headers MUST NOT be longer than 50 chars, and MUST only contain alphanumeric and dash chars, and MUST begin with Sps- not X-.

rules.name: Response Default Content Language En Us
rules.description: Content-Language is optional but MUST default locale to en-US when none provided.

rules.name: Response Define 401
rules.description: Responses should contain common response - 401 (unauthorized).

rules.name: Response Define 404
rules.description: Responses should contain common response - 404 (not found).

rules.name: Response Define 429
rules.description: Responses should contain common response - 429 (too many requests).

rules.name: Response Define 500
rules.description: Responses should contain common response - 500 (server error).

rules.name: Response Delete 404 Use Schema Ref
rules.description: DELETE 404 not found HTTP status codes have a schema references to standardize the response payload returned for the error response.

rules.name: Response Delete Codes Allowed
rules.description: DELETE operations should not return 200 or 201 status codes.

rules.name: Response Delete Define 204
rules.description: DELETE responses should have a 204 success HTTP status codes, communicating a success created response to consumers.

rules.name: Response Delete Define 401
rules.description: DELETE operations should define a 401 Unauthorized response to document authentication requirements for destructive operations.

rules.name: Response Delete Define 500
rules.description: DELETE responses should have a 500 internal server erorr HTTP status code, communicating the API had a problem to consumers.

rules.name: Response Delete Must Not Return Body
rules.description: DELETE operations must not have a response body.

rules.name: Response Delete Require 204
rules.description: All DELETE methods MUST have a 204 response.

rules.name: Response Deprecated Operation Deprecation Header
rules.description: Deprecated operations should document a Deprecation response header (RFC 9745) signalling that the resource is deprecated.

rules.name: Response Deprecated Operation Sunset Header
rules.description: Deprecated operations should document a Sunset response header (RFC 8594) telling clients when the operation will be removed.

rules.name: Response Error Match Problem Schema
rules.description: WARN: This rule is under implementation and just provides an hint. Error management is a key enabler of a resilient API ecosystem. Enforcing a consistent schema for errors between different APIs, enables client to properly implement an error management strategy, with positive impacts for users. This rule inspects the schema returned by an error response and verifies whether it contains the main properties defined in RFC7807: `status`, `title` and `detail`. An example of a valid payload is ``` { "title": "Not Found", "status": 404, "detail": "Book does not exist; id: 123" } ``` See recommendation RAC_REST_NAME_007.

rules.name: Response Error Require Description
rules.description: Check if the response description is appropriate for all requests.

rules.name: Response Error Schema Problem Property Names
rules.description: WARN: This rule is under implementation and just provides an hint. Error management is a key enabler of a resilient API ecosystem. Enforcing a consistent schema for errors between different APIs, enables client to properly implement an error management strategy, with positive impacts for users. Errors should return RFC7807 objects. Instead, this schema seems to use non standard properties such as: `message`, `msg` and `code`. An error of the following form ``` { "msg": "Book with id: 123 does not exist.", "code": 6063 } ``` can be expressed in RFC7807 with ``` { "detail": "Book with id: 123 does not exist.", "type": "https://api.example/v1/errors/6063", "status": 404, "title": "Not Found" } ``` Returning an URI in `type`, instead of an opaque `code` can help the client in better identifying the error; moreover the URI though it should not be dereferenced automatically, can return an actual resource providing guidance in addressing the issue. See recommendation RAC_REST_NAME_007.

rules.name: Response Error Standard Payload
rules.description: All 4XX and 5XX response codes must follow Trimble API Standard.

rules.name: Response Error Use Problem JSON
rules.description: All 4xx and 5xx error responses MUST use the `application/problem+json` media type (RFC 7807).

rules.name: Response Error Use Problem Type
rules.description: Error management is a key enabler of a resilient API ecosystem. Enforcing a consistent schema for errors between different APIs, enables client to properly implement an error management strategy, with positive impacts for users. Error responses should return one of the media-type defined in RFC7807: - `application/problem+json` - `application/problem+xml` An example of a valid response: ``` responses: "503": content: application/problem+json: schema: ... ```.

rules.name: Response Error Use Problem Type 2
rules.description: Every error response SHOULD support RFC 7807.

rules.name: Response Get Cache Control
rules.description: GET success responses should define a Cache-Control header so clients and intermediaries know how to cache the representation.

rules.name: Response Get Codes Allowed
rules.description: GET operations should not use status codes 201, 202, 204, 409, 412.

rules.name: Response Get Define 200
rules.description: GET responses should have a 200 success HTTP status codes, communicating a successful response to consumers.

rules.name: Response Get Define 401
rules.description: GET operations should define a 401 Unauthorized response. Analysis of 773 specs shows 401 is the second most common response code with 4805 occurrences, confirming authentication errors must be documented.

rules.name: Response Get Define 500
rules.description: GET responses should have a 500 internal server erorr HTTP status code, communicating the API had a problem to consumers.

rules.name: Response Get Document 304 Not Modified
rules.description: GET operations should document a 304 Not Modified response so ETag/Last-Modified conditional requests are handled.

rules.name: Response Get ETag
rules.description: GET success responses should define an ETag header to enable validation-based caching and conditional requests.

rules.name: Response Get Last Modified
rules.description: GET success responses should define a Last-Modified header to enable date-based conditional requests.

rules.name: Response Get Link Header
rules.description: GET success responses should provide a Link header (RFC 8288) with rel next/prev/first/last for pagination and navigation — the header-based alternative to an in-body paging object.

rules.name: Response Get Must Return 200
rules.description: GET operations must always return 200 status code.

rules.name: Response Get Must Return Body
rules.description: A `GET` operation must return a response body.

rules.name: Response Get Vary
rules.description: GET success responses should define a Vary header so caches key correctly on the request headers that affect the representation.

rules.name: Response Head Codes Allowed
rules.description: HEAD operations should not return 201, 202, 204, 409, 412 status codes.

rules.name: Response Head Must Not Return Body
rules.description: HEAD operations should not return a response body for success.

rules.name: Response Header Names Pascal Case
rules.description: Headers should be pascal-case. See Italian recommendation RAC_REST_NAME_003.

rules.name: Response Header No X Prefix
rules.description: 'HTTP' headers SHOULD NOT start with 'X-' RFC6648.

rules.name: Response Include Hypermedia Links
rules.description: The API contract MAY include hypermedia links to represent the state of resources and be navigable.

rules.name: Response Include Ratelimit Header
rules.description: Response must include ratelimit-x headers.

rules.name: Response Keys Snake Case
rules.description: response key must be snake cased (e.g. snake_case).

rules.name: Response Location Header Only On 201
rules.description: Location header MUST NOT be present in non-201 responses.

rules.name: Response Must Use JSON Content Type
rules.description: API responses should use application/json as the primary content type. Analysis of 773 specs shows application/json is used by 92% of APIs, making it the universal standard for modern REST APIs.

rules.name: Response Names Pascal Case
rules.description: Response names SHOULD be written in PascalCase.

rules.name: Response No Operational Header In Spec
rules.description: Access-Control-*, Content-Type, and Accept Headers SHOULD NOT be specified explicitly in a spec as it an operational concern.

rules.name: Response No Sensitive Data In Header
rules.description: Headers MUST NOT contain sensitive data.

rules.name: Response Options Codes Allowed
rules.description: OPTIONS operations should not return 201, 202, 409, 412 status codes.

rules.name: Response Options Must Not Return Body
rules.description: OPTIONS operations should not return a response body for success.

rules.name: Response OWASP API3 Define Error Validation
rules.description: Write operations (POST/PUT/PATCH) should define a 400 or 422 validation error response (OWASP API3 — reject malformed or unexpected properties).

rules.name: Response OWASP API8 Define Error 401
rules.description: Operations should define a 401 Unauthorized response (OWASP API8 — security misconfiguration; document auth failures).

rules.name: Response OWASP API8 Define Error 500
rules.description: Operations should define a 500 Internal Server Error response (OWASP API8 — document server-error behavior).

rules.name: Response Patch Codes Allowed
rules.description: PATCH operations should not return 201 status code.

rules.name: Response Patch Define 200
rules.description: PATCH operations should define a 200 OK response for successful partial updates. Analysis shows PATCH is used in 17.7% of APIs.

rules.name: Response Patch Define 400
rules.description: PATCH operations should define a 400 Bad Request response for validation errors on partial update payloads.

rules.name: Response Patch Define 404
rules.description: PATCH operations should define a 404 Not Found response for cases where the resource to be updated does not exist.

rules.name: Response Post Codes Allowed
rules.description: POST operations should not return 412 status codes.

rules.name: Response Post Define 201
rules.description: POST responses should have a 201 success HTTP status codes, communicating a success created response to consumers.

rules.name: Response Post Define 400
rules.description: POST operations should define a 400 Bad Request response for input validation errors. Analysis shows 400 is the 4th most common response code across 2516 operations.

rules.name: Response Post Define 401
rules.description: POST operations should define a 401 Unauthorized response to document authentication requirements for write operations.

rules.name: Response Post Define 500
rules.description: POST responses should have a 500 internal server erorr HTTP status code, communicating the API had a problem to consumers.

rules.name: Response Post Require 201 Or 202
rules.description: All POST methods MUST have a 201 or 202 response.

rules.name: Response Prefer 400 Over 422
rules.description: MUST use standard HTTP status codes [150a].

rules.name: Response Put Codes Allowed
rules.description: PUT operations should not return 200 or 201 status codes.

rules.name: Response Put Define 400
rules.description: PUT responses should have a 400 not found HTTP status code, communicating nothing was found to consumers.

rules.name: Response Put Define 401
rules.description: PUT operations should define a 401 Unauthorized response to document authentication requirements for update operations.

rules.name: Response Put Define 500
rules.description: PUT responses should have a 500 internal server erorr HTTP status code, communicating the API had a problem to consumers.

rules.name: Response Ratelimit Header
rules.description: Responses should define the structured RateLimit header (current IETF draft) conveying quota, remaining, and reset in one field — the modern successor to the RateLimit-Limit/Remaining/Reset triplet.

rules.name: Response Ratelimit Policy
rules.description: Responses should define a RateLimit-Policy header documenting the quota policy and window (e.g. 100;w=60), so clients understand the limits in effect.

rules.name: Response Require Status Health Check Path
rules.description: You must define a `/status` path that can be used to health-check the API. Using this path avoids the arbitrary usage of a server URL for health-check scope. The `/status` endpoint should return a `application/problem+json` response containing a successful status code if the service is working correctly. The service provider is free to define the implementation logic for this path.

rules.name: Response Return Correlation ID
rules.description: Responses should return a correlation / request ID header (e.g. Request-Id or X-Correlation-Id) so callers can correlate a response with their request and with server-side logs.

rules.name: Response Status Path Problem Schema Fields
rules.description: "/status" schema is not a Problem object.

rules.name: Response Status Path Return Problem Type
rules.description: "/status" must return a Problem object.

rules.name: Response Success Require Ratelimit Header
rules.description: Ratelimiting API preserves a service and limits attack scenario [see API4:2019 Lack of Resources & Rate Limiting](https://owasp.org/www-project-api-security). APIs should use the following headers at least on successful responses: - `X-RateLimit-Limit`: number of total requests in a give time window - `X-RateLimit-Remaining`: remaining requests in the current window - `X-RateLimit-Reset`: number of seconds before the window resets An example set of headers is the following ``` X-Ratelimit-Limit: 100 X-Ratelimit-Remaining: 40 X-Ratelimit-Reset: 12 ``` A standardization proposal for ratelimit headers is ongoning inside the IETF HTTPAPI Workgroup. See [the draft](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-httpapi-ratelimit-headers/).

rules.name: Response Success Use HAL JSON
rules.description: All 2xx success responses MUST use the `application/hal+json` media type.

rules.name: Response Throttled Require Retry After
rules.description: When a client is either: * throttled out with a 429 status code; * warned about a temporary server issue with a 503 status code; the server should explicitly communicate how long to wait before issuing further requests using the Retry-After header. Retry-After is defined in RFC7231.

rules.name: Response Use JSON API
rules.description: All responses MUST use the JSON:API `application/vnd.api+json` media type.

rules.name: Response Use Standard Mime Types
rules.description: MIME types MUST be standard (application/json, application/problem+json, application/problem+xml) or use custom format application/vnd.*.

rules.name: Response Use Standard Status Codes
rules.description: MUST use standard HTTP status codes [150a].

rules.name: Response Use Standard Status Codes 2
rules.description: An API MUST return HTTP response codes in conformance with RFC-2616 and common usage.

rules.name: Response Write Document 412 Precondition Failed
rules.description: Write operations should document a 412 Precondition Failed response for failed conditional (If-Match) writes.

rules.name: Response Write ETag
rules.description: Write success responses (POST/PUT/PATCH) should define an ETag header so clients receive the new entity tag after a write and can make subsequent conditional requests.

rules.name: Response Write Require Content Type
rules.description: All API responses MUST include the Content-Type header in all responses.

rules.name: Schema Array Property Require Items
rules.description: Schema properties that are of the type array must have an items property defined.

rules.name: Schema Array Property Require Max Items
rules.description: Schema properties that are of the type array should have a max items property defined.

rules.name: Schema Array Property Require Min Items
rules.description: Schema properties that are of the type array should have a min items property defined.

rules.name: Schema Array Property Typed Items
rules.description: Array properties must have an items attribute with a type.

rules.name: Schema Array Require Min Max Items
rules.description: Array size should be limited to mitigate resource exhaustion attacks. This can be done using `maxItems` and `minItems`, like in the example below. ``` Limited: type: array maxItems: 10 items: type: string format: date ``` You should ensure that the schema referenced in `items` is constrained too. If you delegate input validation to a library or framework, be sure to test it thoroughly and ensure that it verifies `maxItems`.

rules.name: Schema Boolean No Prefix
rules.description: Boolean properties SHOULD NOT use is, has, or another prefix.

rules.name: Schema Collection Require Pagination Params
rules.description: Collection GET endpoints SHOULD support pagination using query parameters. Offset or cursor based pagination is required.

rules.name: Schema Created Datetime Must Be Date Time
rules.description: createdDateTime MUST use a data type of 'string' with the format 'date-time'.

rules.name: Schema Createdby Property Must Be String
rules.description: createdBy MUST use a data type of 'string'.

rules.name: Schema Date Time Property Require Format
rules.description: Date/time string properties (createdAt, updated_at, *_date, timestamp, …) should declare a format such as date-time (RFC 3339) so the value is unambiguous and machine-validatable.

rules.name: Schema Definition Camel Case
rules.description: Schema definitions MUST follow camelCase.

rules.name: Schema Definition Constant Case
rules.description: Schema definitions MUST follow CONSTANT_CASE (screaming snake_case).

rules.name: Schema Definition Kebab Case
rules.description: Schema definitions MUST follow kebab-case.

rules.name: Schema Definition Pascal Case
rules.description: Schema definitions MUST follow PascalCase.

rules.name: Schema Definition Snake Case
rules.description: Schema definitions MUST follow snake_case.

rules.name: Schema Deletedby Property Must Be String
rules.description: deletedBy MUST use a data type of 'string'.

rules.name: Schema Deprecated Property Require Description
rules.description: Deprecated schema properties should have a description explaining the deprecation and the replacement field.

rules.name: Schema Description Max Length
rules.description: Schema should have a length limit applied, restricting how long schema descriptions can be, helping keep them concise and consistent.

rules.name: Schema Description Property Must Be String
rules.description: description MUST use a data type of 'string'.

rules.name: Schema Enum Not Empty
rules.description: An enum should declare at least one value — an empty enum permits nothing and is almost always a mistake.

rules.name: Schema Fingerprint Property Must Be String
rules.description: Fingerprint values MUST use a data type of `string`.

rules.name: Schema Get Define 404 Response
rules.description: GET responses should have a 404 not found HTTP status code, communicating that nothing was found to consumers.

rules.name: Schema ID Property Must Be String
rules.description: id SHOULD use a data type of 'string'.

rules.name: Schema Integer Format Allowed Values
rules.description: To improve interoperability, integer and number formats are constrained to a shared subset. See recommendation RAC_REST_FORMAT_004.

rules.name: Schema Integer Require Format
rules.description: Schema of type number or integer must specify a format to express the associated datatype, eg. `int32`, `int64`, ... You can express similar requirements using the `minimum` and `maximum` properties. See recommendation RAC_REST_FORMAT_004.

rules.name: Schema Keys Snake Case
rules.description: schema key must be snake cased (e.g. snake_case).

rules.name: Schema Modified Datetime Must Be Date Time
rules.description: modifiedDateTime MUST use a data type of 'string' with the format 'date-time'.

rules.name: Schema Modifiedby Property Must Be String
rules.description: modifiedBy MUST use a data type of 'string'.

rules.name: Schema Monetary Property Avoid Float
rules.description: Monetary properties (amount, price, cost, fee, balance, …) should avoid floating-point numbers, which lose precision — use an integer of minor units or a decimal string instead.

rules.name: Schema Name Max Length
rules.description: Schema should have a length limit applied keeping the names of schema consistent across APIs.

rules.name: Schema Name Property Must Be String
rules.description: name MUST use a data type of 'string'.

rules.name: Schema Names Pascal Case
rules.description: Schema names SHOULD be written in PascalCase.

rules.name: Schema Number Format Allowed Values
rules.description: To improve interoperability, integer and number formats are constrained to a shared subset. See recommendation RAC_REST_FORMAT_004.

rules.name: Schema Number Property Require Maximum
rules.description: Schema properties that are of the type number should have a maximum property defined.

rules.name: Schema Number Property Require Minimum
rules.description: Schema properties that are of the type number should have a minimum property defined.

rules.name: Schema Number Require Format
rules.description: Schema of type number or integer must specify a format to express the associated datatype, eg. `int32`, `int64`, ... You can express similar requirements using the `minimum` and `maximum` properties. See recommendation RAC_REST_FORMAT_004.

rules.name: Schema Number Require Min Max
rules.description: Numeric values should be limited in size to mitigate resource exhaustion using `maximum` and `minimum`. If you delegate input validation to a library or framework, be sure to test it thoroughly.

rules.name: Schema Object Constrain Additional Property
rules.description: By default, jsonschema allows additionalProperties. This means that schema validators can be bypassed using further, unspecified fields. While forbidding additionalProperties can create rigidity and hinder the evolution of an API - eg making it hard to accept new parameters or fields - it is possible that this flexibility can be used to bypass the schema validator and force the application to process unwanted information. Disable `additionalProperties` with `false` ``` Person: type: object additionalProperties: false properties: given_name: type: string pattern: [a-zA-Z ]{24} ``` Or constraint them using `maxProperties` ``` Person: type: object additionalProperties: type: string pattern: /+39[0-9]{,14}/ maxProperties: 3 properties: given_name: type: string pattern: [a-zA-Z ]{24} ``` - no additionalProperties - constrained additionalProperties.

rules.name: Schema Object Disallow Additional Property
rules.description: By default, jsonschema allows additionalProperties. This means that schema validators can be bypassed using further, unspecified fields. While forbidding additionalProperties can create rigidity and hinder the evolution of an API - eg making it hard to accept new parameters or fields - it is possible that this flexibility can be used to bypass the schema validator and force the application to process unwanted information. Disable `additionalProperties` with `false` ``` Person: type: object additionalProperties: false properties: given_name: type: string pattern: [a-zA-Z ]{24} ``` Or constraint them using `maxProperties` ``` Person: type: object additionalProperties: type: string pattern: /+39[0-9]{,14}/ maxProperties: 3 properties: given_name: type: string pattern: [a-zA-Z ]{24} ``` - no additionalProperties - constrained additionalProperties.

rules.name: Schema Object Set Additional Property
rules.description: By default, jsonschema allows additionalProperties. This means that schema validators can be bypassed using further, unspecified fields. While forbidding additionalProperties can create rigidity and hinder the evolution of an API - eg making it hard to accept new parameters or fields - it is possible that this flexibility can be used to bypass the schema validator and force the application to process unwanted information. Disable `additionalProperties` with `false` ``` Person: type: object additionalProperties: false properties: given_name: type: string pattern: [a-zA-Z ]{24} ``` Or constraint them using `maxProperties` ``` Person: type: object additionalProperties: type: string pattern: /+39[0-9]{,14}/ maxProperties: 3 properties: given_name: type: string pattern: [a-zA-Z ]{24} ``` - no additionalProperties - constrained additionalProperties. @.additionalProperties)]`).

rules.name: Schema Orgid Property Must Be String
rules.description: orgId MUST use a data type of 'string'.

rules.name: Schema OWASP API4 Integer Format
rules.description: Integer properties should declare a format (int32 / int64) so their range is bounded (OWASP API4 — unrestricted resource consumption).

rules.name: Schema Property Camel Case
rules.description: Schema properties MUST follow camelCase.

rules.name: Schema Property Camel Case 3
rules.description: Schema property names should use camelCase. Analysis of 773 specs shows camelCase is the dominant convention at 65% (12958 properties), followed by snake_case at 33% (6661 properties).

rules.name: Schema Property Constant Case
rules.description: Schema properties MUST follow CONSTANT_CASE (screaming snake_case).

rules.name: Schema Property Description Max Length
rules.description: Schema property descriptions should have a length limit applied, applying constraints to writing descriptions, and keeping consistent across APIs.

rules.name: Schema Property Enum Upper Snake Case
rules.description: Schema property enumerators are consistent casing, keeping all entries upper snake case, and consistent across all APIs.

rules.name: Schema Property Kebab Case
rules.description: Schema properties MUST follow kebab-case.

rules.name: Schema Property Names Camel Case
rules.description: Schema property names are camel case, providing consistent casing across all the schema properties used by APIs.

rules.name: Schema Property Names Camel Case 2
rules.description: Property names and acronyms MUST be in camelCase.

rules.name: Schema Property Names Length Limit
rules.description: Schema property names have a length restriction applied, keeping names consistent, and avoiding being too long.

rules.name: Schema Property No Language Keywords
rules.description: Names that may conflict with keywords in common programming languages SHOULD NOT be used.

rules.name: Schema Property No Prepositions
rules.description: Property names SHOULD NOT include prepositions (e.g. "for", "during", "at", etc.).

rules.name: Schema Property Pascal Case
rules.description: Schema properties MUST follow PascalCase.

rules.name: Schema Property Require Description
rules.description: Schema properties should have descriptions that provide a narrative of the property contains, and how it can be used.

rules.name: Schema Property Require Examples
rules.description: Object properties must include examples.

rules.name: Schema Property Require Type
rules.description: Schema properties must have a type defined.

rules.name: Schema Property Snake Case
rules.description: Schema properties MUST follow snake_case.

rules.name: Schema Ref Property Must Be String
rules.description: ref MUST use a data type of 'string'.

rules.name: Schema Ref Property Require Format
rules.description: Property with the name 'ref' MUST use a format 'sps-ref'.

rules.name: Schema Ref Property Standard
rules.description: Properties following 'sps-ref' format MUST use the standardized schema - maxLength (255), minLength(7), pattern (includes 'sps'), type (string).

rules.name: Schema Requestid Property Must Be String
rules.description: requestId MUST use a data type of 'string'.

rules.name: Schema Require Description
rules.description: Schema should have descriptions that provide a narrative of what a schema object is for, and how it can be used, leaving examples to demonstrate what can actually be expected.

rules.name: Schema Require Info Object
rules.description: Having an info object provides much of the metadata needed for the collection of APIs described in an OpenAPI.

rules.name: Schema Require Name
rules.description: Schema names, keeping the naming of them consistent across APIs, standardizing how consumers can use in their applications.

rules.name: Schema Require Tag Object
rules.description: There needs to be a central tags object applied to the OpenAPI, providing central tags that can be applied across all operations within an OpenAPI.

rules.name: Schema Require Type
rules.description: Schema should have a type defined, being explicit about type of data a schema describes and can be used to validate, helping standardize the type of data being made available.

rules.name: Schema String Property Require Max Length
rules.description: Schema properties that are of the string type have the max length applied defining the shape of the property.

rules.name: Schema String Property Require Min Length
rules.description: Schema properties that are of the string type have the min length applied defining the shape of the property.

rules.name: Schema String Require Max Length
rules.description: String length should be limited to avoid an attacker to send very long strings to your service. You can do this in different ways: - specify a `maxLength` - constraint the possible values with an `enum` - use a constrained `format` like `date` or `date-time`. A constrained string using the `date` format. ``` ConstrainedString: type: string format: date ``` Another constrained string using `maxLength`. You can always add further constraints using a `pattern` or a `format`. ``` ZipCode: type: string maxLength: 5 pattern: '[0-9]{5}' ``` For further security, you can always limit string length even in conjunction with `format` and `pattern`.

rules.name: Schema String Require Pattern Or Format
rules.description: String length should be limited to avoid an attacker to send very long strings to your service. You can do this in different ways: - specify a `maxLength` - constraint the possible values with an `enum` - use a constrained `format` like `date` or `date-time`. A constrained string using the `date` format. ``` ConstrainedString: type: string format: date ``` Another constrained string using `maxLength`. You can always add further constraints using a `pattern` or a `format`. ``` ZipCode: type: string maxLength: 5 pattern: '[0-9]{5}' ``` For further security, you can always limit string length even in conjunction with `format` and `pattern`.

rules.name: Schema Use Fingerprint Not Hash
rules.description: Rather than property names refering to the implementation for 'hash' or 'hashkey', you MUST use the property name 'fingerprint'.

rules.name: Schema Use ID Not Identifier
rules.description: Use abbreviations instead of long form names, i.e. identifier SHOULD BE id.

rules.name: Schema Use Org Not Organization
rules.description: Use abbreviations instead of long form names, i.e. organization SHOULD BE org.

rules.name: Schema Write Operation Require Security
rules.description: Your API should be protected by a `security` rule either at global or operation level. Operations should be protected specially when they are tied to non-idempotent HTTP methods like `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH` and `DELETE`. This is done with one or more non-empty `security` rules. Security rules are defined in the `securityScheme` section. An example of a security rule applied at global level. ``` security: - BasicAuth: [] paths: /books: {} /users: {} securitySchemes: BasicAuth: scheme: http type: basic ``` An example of a security rule applied at operation level, which eventually overrides the global one ``` paths: /books: post: security: - AccessToken: [] securitySchemes: BasicAuth: scheme: http type: basic AccessToken: scheme: http type: bearer bearerFormat: JWT ```.

rules.name: Security Bearer Scheme Require Bearer Format
rules.description: HTTP bearer security schemes should declare a bearerFormat (e.g. JWT) so consumers know the token type and tooling can validate it.

rules.name: Security JWT Scheme Require Description
rules.description: Json Web Tokens RFC7519 is a compact, URL-safe means of representing claims to be transferred between two parties. JWT can be enclosed in encrypted or signed tokens like JWS and JWE. The [JOSE IANA registry](https://www.iana.org/assignments/jose/jose.xhtml) provides algorithms information. RFC8725 describes common pitfalls in the JWx specifications and in their implementations, such as: - the ability to ignore algorithms, eg. `{"alg": "none"}`; - using insecure algorithms like `RSASSA-PKCS1-v1_5` eg. `{"alg": "RS256"}`. An API using JWT should explicit in the `description` that the implementation conforms to RFC8725. ``` components: securitySchemes: JWTBearer: type: http scheme: bearer bearerFormat: JWT description: |- A bearer token in the format of a JWS and conformato to the specifications included in RFC8725. ```.

rules.name: Security No HTTP Basic Auth
rules.description: Consider a more secure alternative to HTTP Basic.

rules.name: Security OAuth Disallow Insecure Flows
rules.description: The OAuth2 authorization framework defines various [grant types](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-1.3), most notably the [AuthorizationCode](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-1.3.1) and the [Client Credentials](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-1.3.4). Some grant types are now considered insecure and MUST not be used, including `implicit` and `password`. The new [OAuth2.1](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1-01) still in draft, removes them and suggests to replace the `implicit` with `authorizationCode` + PKCE defined in RFC7636.

rules.name: Security OAuth Endpoints Require HTTPS
rules.description: OAuth2 endpoints must use `https://`.

rules.name: Security OAuth Flow Require Scopes
rules.description: OAuth2 flows should define scopes so authorization is granular and least-privilege rather than all-or-nothing.

rules.name: Security OAuth Scheme Require Description
rules.description: Json Web Tokens RFC7519 is a compact, URL-safe means of representing claims to be transferred between two parties. JWT can be enclosed in encrypted or signed tokens like JWS and JWE. The [JOSE IANA registry](https://www.iana.org/assignments/jose/jose.xhtml) provides algorithms information. RFC8725 describes common pitfalls in the JWx specifications and in their implementations, such as: - the ability to ignore algorithms, eg. `{"alg": "none"}`; - using insecure algorithms like `RSASSA-PKCS1-v1_5` eg. `{"alg": "RS256"}`. An API using JWT should explicit in the `description` that the implementation conforms to RFC8725. ``` components: securitySchemes: JWTBearer: type: http scheme: bearer bearerFormat: JWT description: |- A bearer token in the format of a JWS and conformato to the specifications included in RFC8725. ```.

rules.name: Security OpenID-Connect Require URL
rules.description: OpenID Connect security schemes should declare an openIdConnectUrl pointing at the discovery document so clients can configure themselves.

rules.name: Security OpenID-Connect URL HTTPS
rules.description: The OpenID Connect discovery URL (openIdConnectUrl) should use https so token/identity configuration is not retrieved over an insecure channel.

rules.name: Security OWASP API2 Auth Insecure Schemes
rules.description: HTTP auth schemes should not use insecure mechanisms such as negotiate or oauth1 (OWASP API2). Prefer bearer with a vetted token.

rules.name: Security OWASP API2 No API Keys In URL
rules.description: API key security schemes should be sent in a header or cookie, never in the query string or path (OWASP API2 — credentials leak into logs and history).

rules.name: Security Require Root
rules.description: The API contract MUST include a 'security' section at the root level.

rules.name: Security Require Root Scheme
rules.description: Security field MUST be present at the root of the spec with at least one item (ie. HTTPBearer, Token, APIKey, etc.).

rules.name: Security Scheme Require Description
rules.description: Every security scheme should declare a description so consumers know how to authenticate (which token, where to get it, what scopes).

rules.name: Server Must Not Specify Port
rules.description: Port MUST NOT be specified or required to use the API, except for 'localhost' testing in a spec.

rules.name: Server Must Use HTTPS
rules.description: Servers MUST be https and no other protocol is allowed unless using localhost.

rules.name: Server On Approved Domain
rules.description: APIs SHOULD be accessible under api.spscommerce.com.

rules.name: Server Require Description
rules.description: Servers must have a description.

rules.name: Server Require HTTPS
rules.description: ALL requests MUST go through `https` protocol only.

rules.name: Server Require HTTPS 2
rules.description: Servers must use https to ensure the origin of the responses and protect the integrity and the confidentiality of the communication. You can use `http://` only on sandboxes environment. Use `x-sandbox: true` to skip this kind of check.

rules.name: Server URL Include Version
rules.description: API URLs versions should meet the Trimble API Standards.

rules.name: Server URL Meet Standards
rules.description: API URLs should meet the Trimble API Standards.

rules.name: Server URL Must Be Lowercase
rules.description: Server URL SHOULD BE lowercase.

rules.name: Server URL Require HTTPS
rules.description: API server URLs should use HTTPS to ensure encrypted communication between clients and servers, protecting sensitive data in transit.

rules.name: Tag Description
rules.description: Tag object must have "description".

rules.name: Tag Names Title Case
rules.description: The first letter of each word in a tag being applied to APIs needs to be capitalized, keeping the tags being applied across APIs the same look and feel for organizing and publishing to documentation.

rules.name: Tag No Camel Case
rules.description: Detects a tag with camelCase (personalItem) text within it.

rules.name: Tag No Pascal Case
rules.description: Tag names cannot use Pascal Case. Please rename {{value}}.

rules.name: Tag No Version Info
rules.description: Tag names cannot have version information {{value}}. Consider versioning your API.

rules.name: Tag Require At Least One
rules.description: There needs to be at least one tag applied to an OpenAPI, providing a key word or phrase that can be applied to API operations.

rules.name: Tag Require Name
rules.description: Tags used as part of an OpenAPI should have names, providing a simple key word or phrase that represents the tag being applied to APIs.

rules.name: Typed Enum
rules.description: Enum values must respect the specified type.

apis-json.label: APIs.json
